title: Lavender body lotion
subtitle: A light, emulsified body lotion with sweet almond oil, shea butter, and lavender essential oil
excerpt: A standard o/w body lotion: about 70% water, 20% oils and butters, and 6% emulsifying wax to hold the two phases together. Lavender essential oil at 1.5% gives a clean floral scent. Making an emulsified lotion is intermediate work because both phases must be heated separately to the same temperature before combining.
sourceNotes: Standard o/w emulsion body lotion. Water phase 68 to 70%; oil phase 20 to 22%; emulsifying wax NF 5 to 7%. Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) essential oil at 1.5% for body lotion (IFRA limit for rinse-off much higher; leave-on 1.5% is well within limit). Preservative 1% (Optiphen or equivalent). Both phases heated to 70 to 75°C. Stick blender for initial emulsification.

recipe.yieldDescription: ~100 g body lotion (fills a 100 ml pump bottle)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale all phases proportionally. Keep emulsifying wax at 6% and preservative at 1%.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow the lotion to cool fully before pumping into a bottle, about 1 hour.
recipe.temperatureNote: Heat both phases to 70 to 75°C before combining. Temperature parity prevents the emulsion breaking.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months at room temperature with preservative. Keep the pump closed. Discard if the lotion separates or smells rancid.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of combining an oil phase and a water phase into a stable, uniform mixture using an emulsifying agent. In a lotion, the emulsifier surrounds oil droplets and keeps them suspended in water, preventing separation.
Both are emulsions of oil and water. Lotions have a higher water content (60 to 75%) and are more fluid; creams have more oil (30 to 45%) and are thicker. This recipe is a lotion.

content.text: A body lotion is a water-in-oil
: roughly 70% water phase and 20% oil phase, held together by emulsifying wax. The resulting product is a

: fluid, not stiff. Both phases must reach the same temperature before you combine them; too large a temperature gap causes the emulsion to break and the lotion to separate on cooling. Lavender essential oil and preservative go in last, below 40°C.

items.prepNote: Water phase; distilled or freshly boiled and cooled
Optional; antioxidant for the oil phase

content.text: In the larger beaker (water phase): weigh distilled water and glycerin. In the smaller beaker (oil phase): weigh shea butter, sweet almond oil, jojoba oil, and emulsifying wax. Place both in a simmering-water bath and heat to 72°C, monitoring both with the probe thermometer.

With both phases at 70 to 75°C, pour the oil phase slowly into the water phase while blending with the stick blender at low speed. Blend for 2 minutes until the mixture turns white and thickens. Stir continuously as it cools.
When the lotion has cooled to below 40°C, add the lavender essential oil and preservative. Stir well. Transfer to a pump bottle.
3 months at room temperature. Keep the pump closed. Discard if the lotion separates, smells rancid, or develops an off colour.
